coredata.dc:title: A comparative study of Fe (II) doped chiral L/D-methionine carbon dots (MCDs) as a “Turn-on” fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of L – cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Abraham, Merin K
R, Jithin Raj
Madanan, Anju S
Varghese, Susan
Shkhair, Ali Ibrahim
Indongo, Geneva
Rajeevan, Greeshma
Vijila, N.S.
Arathy, B.K.
George, Sony
coredata.dc:description: Chiral carbon dots have prominently influenced in the field of chemical, biological, and medicinal research. Comparing to achiral carbon dots, chiral carbon dots exhibit unique characteristics in biosensing application. In this perspective, we have developed bright blue, fluorescent L and d-Methionine Chiral carbon dots (MCDs) using a simple microwave assisted process for effective sensing of l-Cysteine. l-Cysteine plays a vital role in balancing the physiological redox in the body. These MCDs can be complexed with Fe2+ ions to create an energy transfer quenching system due to distinctive N and S functional group distribution on their surface. However, l-Cysteine attaches to Fe2+ ions in a competitive manner, leads to recovery of MCDs fluorescence. As a result, a "turn-on" fluorescent sensing platform based on L and d-Methionine-CDs has been developed for the rapid identification and detection of l-Cysteine. The probe shows good selectivity and sensitivity with coexisting biomolecules and ions. From comparative PL studies, d- MCDs are more responsive than l-Meth CDs towards the detection of l-cysteine with LOD of 0.71 µM and 0.94 µM respectively. The chiroptical properties were evaluated and the probe extends its practical application in paper strip and in biological matrix such as human blood serum with satisfactory recoveries in the range 85–104%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dot
Fluorescence
Methionine
Paper strip assay
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of chiral carbon dots (L/D methionine CDs)
Synthesis and characterisation of chiral carbon dots (L-MCDs and d-MCDs)
Fluorescence “Turn-off” by the addition of Fe (II)
The “Turn-on” sensing of l-Cysteine using l-MCDs and d-MCDs
Plausible mechanism of detection of l-Cysteine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: International Interuniversity Centre for Sensing and Imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author thanks the Head, Department of Chemistry, University of Kerala, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala for the support, guidance, and instrumental facilities. We are also thankful to CSIR-NIIST, Thiruvananthapuram and CLIF, University of Kerala for providing instrumental facilities. We also thank the International Interuniversity Centre for Sensing and Imaging (IIUCSI), Advanced Research Laboratory for Molecular Sensing and Imaging, State Plan Fund 2020\u201321, University of Kerala for the sophisticated instrumental analysis offered.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of A. l-methionine CDs (0.02 g/mL) and B.D-Methionine CDs (0.02 g/mL) under UV light and Visible light.

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of synthesised l-MCDs and B. D-MCDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 400 nm. C. The excitation and emission spectra of l-MCDs and d-d-MCDs respectively.

A. The SEM image of synthesised l-MCDs, B. d-MCDs, C. The XRD spectra of l-MCDs, D. d-MCDs, E. The EDS spectra of l-MCDs and F. D-MCDs, G. ATR-FTIR spectra of l- methionine (red), citric acid(green), urea(blue), L Methionine CDs (brown) and, D methionine (magenta) and d- Methionine CDs (pink) respectively.
A. Circular Dichroism spectra of l- methionine (red) and B. D Methionine precursor (violet) alone and synthesised C. l- methionine CDs and D. d-Methionine CDs respectively.
A. Fluorescence “Turn-off” response of l- MCDs and B. D-MCDs towards increasing concentrations of 0.1 M (FeCl2) solution (0.6- 4.7 mM) at λex = 380 nm, λem = 450 nm respectively.
Fluorescence “Turn-on” response of A. L-MCDs-Fe (II), B. d-MCDs-Fe (II) system towards increasing concentrations of 0.1 M l-Cysteine at λex = 380 nm, λem = 450 nm respectively.
Schematic Representation of Turn-on sensing of l-Cysteine based on l-Methionine and d-Methionine Carbon dots (MCDs).
A. The UV Visible absorption spectra of a. L methionine CDs, d-Methionine CD+ Fe, l- Methionine CD+ Fe+ l-Cysteine, B. D Methionine CD, D Methionine CD+ Fe, D Methionine CD+ Fe+ l-Cysteine.
A. The time resolved fluorescence decay profile of l-MCDs (red), l-MCDs +Fe (yellow) and L MCDs+ Fe + l-cysteine (green), B. d-MCDs (red), d-MCDs +Fe (Blue) and d-MCDs+ Fe + l-cysteine (green).
A. The linear response plot of intensity (I0/I) against different concentration of l-Cysteine in l-MCDs and B. in d-MCDs.

A. The interference bar diagram of l-Cysteine towards l-Methionine CDs and B. d-methionine CDs in the presence of various coexisting metal ions (Black bar represents interfering metal ions and the red bar represents “turn-on” response of in the presence of interfering biomolecules.
The paper strip observed at day light (left) and under UV light (right) Whatman No.40 filter paper coated with MCDs, quenched with iron and “Turn on” by using L cysteine.
The spiked human serum sample for detection of l-Cysteine.

head.ce:title: A comparative study of Fe (II) doped chiral L/D-methionine carbon dots (MCDs) as a “Turn-on” fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of L – cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Research Centre, University of Kerala, Kariavattom campus, Thiruvananthapuram – 695581, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Research Centre, University of Kerala, Kariavattom campus, Thiruvananthapuram – 695581, Kerala, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Research Centre, University of Kerala, Kariavattom campus, Thiruvananthapuram – 695581, Kerala, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral carbon dots have prominently influenced in the field of chemical, biological, and medicinal research. Comparing to achiral carbon dots, chiral carbon dots exhibit unique characteristics in biosensing application. In this perspective, we have developed bright blue, fluorescent L and d-Methionine Chiral carbon dots (MCDs) using a simple microwave assisted process for effective sensing of l-Cysteine. l-Cysteine plays a vital role in balancing the physiological redox in the body. These MCDs can be complexed with Fe2+ ions to create an energy transfer quenching system due to distinctive N and S functional group distribution on their surface. However, l-Cysteine attaches to Fe2+ ions in a competitive manner, leads to recovery of MCDs fluorescence. As a result, a "turn-on" fluorescent sensing platform based on L and d-Methionine-CDs has been developed for the rapid identification and detection of l-Cysteine. The probe shows good selectivity and sensitivity with coexisting biomolecules and ions. From comparative PL studies, d- MCDs are more responsive than l-Meth CDs towards the detection of l-cysteine with LOD of 0.71 µM and 0.94 µM respectively. The chiroptical properties were evaluated and the probe extends its practical application in paper strip and in biological matrix such as human blood serum with satisfactory recoveries in the range 85–104%.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: L-Cysteine also known as 2-Amino-3-sulfhydrylpropanoic acid is a proteinogenic amino acid. The thiol side chain in cysteine actively participates in enzymatic reactions as a nucleophile [1]. The thiol is susceptible to oxidation to give the disulfide derivative cystine, which is a kind of covalent bond that has key role in folding and stabilisation of the tertiary structure of proteins sustaining the biological functions [2]. It is a hydrophilic amino acid that contains thiol groups which is essential for sustaining biological redox equilibrium, metabolism, protein metabolism detoxification of blood, and intracellular signalling that controls gene expression. The body makes l-cysteine from another amino acid called methionine and it is naturally made by the body from food consumed in the diet. It has antioxidant effects and is used to make up antioxidants such as glutathione. The normal level of L Cysteine in human blood is 200-300 µmol/L. Above 300 µmol/L is considered as diseased condition [3]. This amino acid is mainly involved in maintaining the homeostasis of physiological redox in the body. L-cysteine in the biological system leads to various diseases such as cancer, diabetes, anemia, heart failure, stroke, and many other conditions. Therefore, rapid identification and quantification of L cysteine in human body is an essential factor in early diagnosis of many diseases. Currently, some traditional analytical methods have been used to quantitatively detect l-cysteine, including capillary electrophoresis [4], colorimetry [5], electrochemistry [6], high-performance liquid chromatography [7], mass spectrometry [8], and fluorescence analysis methods [9]. However, there are still numerous drawbacks and restrictions with these methods. In contrast, fluorescence detection methods benefit from having real-time detection, good reliability, and easy operation procedures.

Nature is encompassed of homochiral molecules and materials. Indeed, all living systems are build-up of chiral l-amino acids, and proteins, enzymes as well as sugars in the backbone of DNA and RNA [10]. Chirality is a term mainly used in the field of geometry to refer to the property that the mirror image of an object cannot coincide with itself. Chiral molecules have resemblance in many physicochemical properties due to mirror symmetry, but because of the incapability in complete overlap they show some difference in chemical properties mainly in some biochemical reactions in living organisms [11]. Chirality in carbon dots can remain a critical consideration in drug development and design, and in applications of enantioselective recognition and sensing. At first, chiral quantum dots are highly preferred by the researchers, but the high cost and toxicity limited their application [12]. In recent years chiral carbon dots as a new type of nanomaterial they have superior optical properties like chiral quantum dots but also adapt low toxicity and good biocompatibility of carbon materials, easy functionalisation, large scale synthesis and low price. Chiral carbon dots are a novel luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with chirality. It has uniform spherical shapes with varying diameters ranging from 2 to 22 nm [13]. Bin Yang has created a new cluster-based Ca (II)-Metal organic frameworks material synthesised under the solvothermal synthetic conditions, which possessed good water dispersibility, excellent photoluminescence properties, and great fluorescence stability. An “off−on” fluorescence sensor was successfully designed for l-cysteine determination in biological fluids [14]. Huang et al. has designed a switch on fluorescence mode for determination of l-cysteine with carbon quantum dots and Au nanoparticles as a probe. Chen et al. fabricated a turn off sensor for the detection of serum l- Cysteine based on AuNC on AuNPs ensembles. They developed a fluorescence “turn-off-on” probe based on AuNCs AuNPs ensembles, which detected l-cysteine with high selectivity [15]. Zhang et al. designed a fluorescent probe for “off – on” sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and l-cysteine based on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets. A novel approach for preparation of graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (CNNS) from stripping graphitic carbon nitride by strong acid and ultrasonic technology was demonstrated in this study for the first time [16]. Singh et al. created a nanosheets for calorimetric and fluorescence sensing of L – cysteine. The utilisation of ultrathin Mn-metal–organic framework (Mn-UMOF) nanosheets for oxidase-like nanozyme activity has been demonstrated [17]. Recently, Yang et al. reported a novel method for the one-pot facile synthesis of sulphur quantum dots (SQDs) based on a dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) accelerated H2O2 etching strategy within merely 20 min. The formation mechanism of SQDs was investigated, with which an “on off on” fluorescence sensor was developed for the detection of lead and l-cysteine [18].

In this work, we have developed bright blue, fluorescent L and d-Methionine Chiral carbon dots (MCDs) separately using a simple microwave assisted process for effective sensing of l-Cysteine. l-Cysteine plays a vital role in balancing the physiological redox in the body. These MCDs can be complexed with Fe2+ ions to create an energy transfer quenching system due to distinctive Nitrogen and Sulfur functional group distribution on their surface moieties. However, l-Cysteine binds to Fe2+ ions in a competitive manner, leads to recovery of MCDs fluorescence. As a result, a "turn-on" fluorescent sensing platform based on L and d-Methionine-CDs has been developed for the rapid identification and detection of l-Cysteine. The probe shows good selectivity and sensitivity with coexisting biomolecules and ions. From comparative PL studies, d- MCDs are more responsive than l-Meth CDs towards the detection of l-cysteine with LOD of 0.71 µM and 0.94 µM respectively. The chiroptical properties were evaluated and the probe extends its practical application in paper strip and in biological matrix such as human blood serum with satisfactory recoveries in the range 85–104%.

Citric acid was purchased from Avantor performance materials India Ltd. Urea, Potassium chloride and sodium hydroxide pellets were brought from Nice Chemicals Pvt Ltd. l-Methionine, d-Methionine l-Cysteine and d-Cysteine were purchased from TCI chemicals Private Limited, Mumbai India. Glucose is purchased from Priya Laboratory reagents Pvt Ltd. Mercuric Chloride is bought from Fischer inorganic and aromatics Ltd, India. Iron chloride, Creatine, Dopamine, Calcium chloride, Sodium chloride, zinc chloride, urea, Homocysteine, histidine, glutathione was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Pvt Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade as received. The experiments were conducted by using ultrapure distilled water.

The Duetta Horiba Scientific Fluorescence and Absorbance Spectrophotometer was used to examine the room temperature photo luminescence and UV Visible absorption of all prepared samples. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer from Agilent techniques, is used to perform the functional group analysis (FT-IR). Lifetime measurements and Quantum yield were carried out using Fluorescence lifetime Spectrofluorometer (FLS 1000) from Edinburg Instruments by using EPLED 450 nm as the excitation source and Integrating Sphere. The morphological and elemental analysis such as Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray Analyzer (EDX) were done in Carl Zeiss EVO 18 Research. The XRD analysis was done using Bruker D8 ADVANCE with DAVINCI design. Horiba S Z-100 instrument was used for Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and zeta potential. pH was adjusted with Scientific Tech. Digital pH meters. The synthesis is done in domestic microwave oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of chiral carbon dots (L/D methionine CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The chiral l-methionine and d-methionine carbon dots were synthesised by using 1.5 g citric acid,1.5 g urea and 0.1 g of l-methionine or 0.1 g of d-methionine separately. The precursors stirred well with 25 mL distilled water and heated in a microwave oven at 500 W for 3-4 min until we get a yellow syrupy mass of 1.34 g (L-MCDs) and 1.48 g (D-MCDs). Then diluted the mass with 15 ml distilled water. The fluorescence is checked under irradiation in UV chamber, a bright blue fluorescence is observed and light-yellow colour under visible light. The fluorescence emission spectra is taken for the as synthesised l-MCDs and d-MCDs respectively. All experiments were conducted in triplicate.

To 3 ml of the l- methionine carbon dot taken in a cuvette. Dilution study was conducted. To that we add 20 µL,40 µL, 60 µL up to 100 µL of 0.1 M of FeCl2, we can see that the intensity of the carbon dot decreases. The fluorescence measurements are recorded. The same procedure is repeated for d-Methionine carbon dots.

About 0.1 M L cysteine was prepared in distilled water. About 3 ml of l-MCDs and 40 µl Fe (II) solution is taken in the cuvette and its fluorescence is measured in spectrofluorometer. To this solution, 10 µl,20 µl incremental addition up to 100 µl of 0.1 M L cysteine is added and shaken well. The PL spectra is taken after 2 min incubation time. The sensing studies of l-Cysteine were also performed in d-methionine carbon dots in similar manner. The selectivity and interference study were done for both l-MCDs and d-MCDs with metal ions such as Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, and coexisting biomolecules such as creatine, glucose, histidine, glutathione, homocysteine, dopamine, urea etc.

Whatman No:40 filter paper was used to conduct the paper strip assay experiment. It was cut into three equal parts. Then, it is immersed in synthesised MCDs that are dried and kept under UV. To this system, 40 µl of 0.1 M Fe (II) solution is added and soaked and dried. Again, to the quenched system, 100 µl of L cysteine is added and soaked, dried and observed under UV chamber. The photographs are taken separately under day light and UV light.

Human blood serum was collected from healthy volunteers. The developed probe was studied with concentrated serum, 10 times and 100 times diluted serum samples. The experiments were done following the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) ethical standards and were approved by the University-level Human Ethical Committee Order no: (ULECRIHS/UOK/2019/48, University of Kerala, Thiruvananthapuram).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterisation of chiral carbon dots (L-MCDs and d-MCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of chiral fluorescent carbon dots (MCDs) was done by microwave irradiation at 500 W with minor modification from previous reports [19]. Herein, Urea (1.5 g) acted as nitrogen source and citric acid (1.5 g) as carbon source. In order to incorporate the chiral properties for the CDs, in situ addition of L and d-methionine amino acids (0.1 g each) are done. During the synthesis, the mixture is magnetically stirred for 5 min until the clear solution is obtained. Then, the mixture is microwave irradiated for 4–5 min until a brown syrupy solution is obtained. The syrupy mass is diluted to 15 ml and the fluorescence is checked under UV chamber. The pH of the obtained l-Methionine CD and d-methionine CDs are acidic in nature with value of 5.6 and 4.06 respectively. The pH studies of l-MCDs and d-MCDs ranging from pH value of 2–12 and the corresponding fluorescence response and the digital photographs of both CDs at different pHs are given in Figs. S1 A and B and S2. respectively. It is observed that the CDs show high fluorescence intensity at synthesised pH (acidic) and significant change in fluorescence intensity with change in pH is detected which is attributed by reversible protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl functional groups present on the surface of carbon dots [20]. When the pH value is increased, the -COOH groups are deprotonated, while the phenolic hydroxyl groups also undergo a gradual deprotonation which result in decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs [21]. The prepared MCDs exhibits bright blue fluorescence at UV light and pale-yellow colour under visible light as shown in Fig. 3.1 A and B.

The synthesised l-MCDs and d-MCDs were further conducted for photoluminescence studies. To evaluate the maximum intensity, the MCDs were subjected to different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340-400 nm as shown in Fig. 3.2 A and B. The maximum intensity was obtained at an excitation at 380 nm with emission wavelength at 450 nm for l-MCDs and d-MCDs. The excitation and emission spectra of synthesised l-MCDs and d-MCDS were taken as given in Fig. 3.2 C and D. Since for both MCDs, excitation at 380 nm possess the maximum intensity. Therefore, 380 nm was selected for further studies in all experiments. It is observed that when subjected to excitation at 380 nm, both MCDs exhibit an excitation dependent emission which is in variation from Kasha-Vavilov rule. This variation can be ascribed due to the ensemble of carbon dots with different diameters with distinct energy gaps that may lead to excitation dependent emission phenomenon [22].

Further, the dilution study of developed carbon dots was conducted at different dilution. Since, distilled water is the solvent for the CDs, the dilution is done in such a fashion as 1:2,1:3,1:4 etc. up to which the intensity of MCDs will enter to a maximum level and further addition would decrease the intensity. Hence, the dilution is fixed each time before the sensing studies were evaluated. The dilution spectra of l-MCDs are obtained as 1:4 ratio and for d-MCDs as 1:3 ratio as given in Fig. S3 A and B. Here, we have to consider the contribution of disaggregation induced enhancement of CD during dilution [23]. Initially, the concentration is obtained as 0.08 g/mL for l-MCDs and 0.06 g/mL for d-MCDs. While the concentration of l-MCDs and d-MCDs during dilution of 1:4 and 1:3 were found to be same concentration of 0.02 g/mL. The quantum yield of l-MCDs and d-MCDs were obtained as 7.4% and 8.8% respectively. The ionic stability of l-MCDs and d-MCDs were evaluated in different concentration of NaCl (0.05–0.1 M) as given in Fig. S1 C and D. The result explains that the MCDs intensity is not much affected by the increasing concentration of NaCl which confirms the stability of chiral MCDs. The temperature dependence study on both MCDs were observed and shown in Fig. S1 E and F. The temperature of l-MCDs and d-MCDs decreases with increase in temperature due to photoexcited electron relaxations in carbon dots, non-radiative relaxations, and thermal activated trapping in surface sites [24]. Furthermore, the photostability of l-MCDs and d-MCDs were evaluated under different irradiation time from 0–100 min as given in Fig. S1 G and H. It is observed that the synthesised MCDs shows stable fluorescence properties which depicts the high photostability of the carbon dots under 365 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter of l-methionine CDs and d-methionine CDs were taken using Dynamic Light Scattering study and obtained as ∼8.8 nm and ∼13.8 nm respectively as shown in Fig. S4 A and B. The increase in size of nanoparticles is due to dilation of outermost water layer on the surface of synthesised MCDs as the MCDs are dispersed in water medium [25]. The surface charge potential of synthesised l-MCDs and d-MCDs were evaluated by zeta potential graph as depicted in Fig. S5 A and B and we obtained a negative potential of −1.8 mV and positive potential of +0.9 mV which confirms the presence of carboxyl functionalised groups on the surface of MCDs, colloidal stability and chiral property of the material. The morphological nature of l-MCDs and d-MCDs were analysed by Scanning Electron Microscope and X-ray Diffraction spectroscopy (XRD). From SEM analysis, the l-MCDs and d-MCDs obtained an agglomerated spherical shaped structure with average size of 100 nm as depicted in Fig. 3.3 A and B [26,27]. Hence, the size obtained can be correlated with DLS particle size of CDs [28]. The XRD results exhibits broad, diffused spectrum without any specific sharp peaks indicating the amorphous nature of synthesised MCDs. as given in Fig. 3.3 C and D [29] The elemental composition of l-MCDs and d-MCDs were characterised by using Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX). The result depicts that l-MCDs are composed of carbon (66.08%), Nitrogen (1.99%), oxygen (37.72%) and sulphur (0.43%) whereas d-MCDs are composed of carbon (41.87%), nitrogen (11.29%), oxygen (43.71%) and sulphur (3.18%) respectively as shown in Fig. 3.3 E and F. The peak observed at 1.7 keV corresponds to Si, since the CDs are coated on the surface of glass plate [30].

The FT-IR spectra is used to evaluate the surface functional groups present in the developed nanomaterial. The FT-IR spectra of synthesised l-Methionine CDs and d-Methionine CDs are taken as given in Fig. 3.3 G. The l-methionine spectra shows peaks at (1557 cm−1) and (1505 cm−1) of corresponding to NH3+and COO− that is present l-methionine structure and d-Methionine spectra also shows peaks at (1557 cm−1) and (1505 cm−1). The broad peaks between 2749cm−1 and 3274 cm−1 for citric acid indicate the presence of OH and NH stretching vibrations. The strong signals at 1711 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1 or 1719cm−1 and 1622 cm−1 are assigned to C = O and C = N stretching modes. The peak at 1378 cm−1 is assigned to C–N stretching modes. The FT-IR spectrum of urea shows a set of distinct absorption bands at 3474 cm−1, which are assigned to N–H stretching vibrations typically observed for primary amines. The two sharp peaks at 1583 and 1453 cm−1 originate from C = O and C- N stretching vibration modes. The peaks at 883–900 cm−1 reveals C-S-C asymmetric stretching. The FT-IR results reveal that L and d-methionine carbon dots have been successfully synthesised from L and d-methionine, citric acid and urea and the surface of MCDs is enriched with COO- and NH2 functional groups from the precursor moiety [31]. The FT-IR result reveals the C-S-C stretching frequencies attributed from l-Methionine and d-Methionine amino acid (precursors) got diffused and disappeared on formation of carbon dots, which proves the effective anchoring of amino acid on the surface of l-MCDs and d-MCDs. Moreover, the result reveals that the surface of MCDs are hydrophilic and enriched with carboxyl, amino groups [32].

The chiroptical properties of the CDs were investigated by circular dichroism spectral-l-methionine CDs and d-methionine CDs produced corresponding mirror image circular dichroism scans with negative and positive cotton effect. The presence of optical activity may be due to the chiral core of the CDs or the chirality of the in-situ structure amino acids in MCDs. The CD spectra is given in Fig. 3.4 A–D. It is observed that the l-Methionine and d-methionine precursors produce a peak at 270 nm and 250 nm which originates from their difference in specific rotation in positive and negative direction. In l-MCDs and d-MCDs, the configurations of the groups present on the surface are different hence their specific rotations are also different. The red shift of peak in both MCDs derived from the corresponding precursor molecules is because of absorption flattering and circular differential scattering mechanism attributed in core of amino acid [33,34]. The peak is retained in the synthesised l-MCDs and d-MCDs but shifting is observed which may be due to aggregation as we have synthesised using a bottom-up approach from parent biomolecules [35]. The spectra show positive and negative peaks for l-MCDs and d-MCDs which confirms that the chirality is retained in prepared MCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “Turn-off” by the addition of Fe (II)
ce:section.ce:para: When 0.1 M of Iron chloride (FeCl2) is added to the synthesised MCDs. The fluorescence intensity is quenched on addition of increasing concentration of different volumes (20μl, 40μl,60μl etc. to 100 µl) of FeCl2 solution. The synthesised MCDs fluorescence intensity spectra is responsive to 4.7 mM (170 μl) of FeCl2. The fluorescence spectra of MCDs towards different concentration of FeCl2 is taken and the “turn-off” behaviour is studied as given in Fig. 3.5 A and B. The fluorescence of MCDs were half the intensity at 40 µl Fe (II) inti the system, hence this concentration is used for the sensing studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The “Turn-on” sensing of l-Cysteine using l-MCDs and d-MCDs
ce:section.ce:para: When 0.1 M of l-cysteine is added incrementally to the developed l-MCDs and d-MCDs, the fluorescence intensity increases with increasing concentration of l-Cysteine. (0 −3.45 mM). To the mixture of L -MCDs and d-MCDs taken separately with 40 µl Fe 2+ (1.3 mM) taken in the cuvette, add 10 μl,20 μl,30 μl,40 μl,50 µL up to 100 μl of l-cysteine, we can see that the intensity of MCDs increases as shown in Fig. 3.6 A and B. The fluorescence intensity is enhanced. The developed sensor shows good response with 3.45 mM (100 μl) of l-Cysteine. The fluorescence spectra of d-MCDs towards different concentration of l-Cysteine (0 mM-3.45 mM) is given in Fig. 3.6 A and B. respectively. When compared to l-methionine carbon dot-Fe (II) system, d-methionine -Fe (II) derived carbon dot system shows enhanced “turn-on” behaviour towards l-Cysteine during photoluminescence studies with similar sensing conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism of detection of l-Cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine is a naturally occurring thiol containing amino acid, having unique and structural properties to the activity of biomolecules. It dynamically participates in electron transfer reactions taking place in heme proteins, ferredoxins, and rubredoxins. l-Cysteine is one of the pivotal bio thiols, and Fe (II) is also a crucial trace element. They play a vital role in human physiological and metabolic processes. Moreover, l-Cysteine has a high affinity for Fe2+ ions, which results in a “turn-on” fluorescent sensing platform for the quantitative detection of l-Cysteine. It contains a variety of functional moieties like carboxyl, thiols and amines. With three potential binding groups, cysteine is, a “complex” ligand which can strongly bond to Fe metal ion. Methionine doping provides N and S resulting in the presence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs. The synthesised MCDs can specifically recognise Fe2+ ions because the surface of MCDs is enriched in nitrogen and sulfur functional groups, which can effectively complex with Fe2+ ions. Due to the formation of MCDs/Fe2+ complexes, the electrons that promote the excited state of the MCDs are transferred to the partly filled orbit of the Fe2+ ions, and therefore, the process of returning excited state electron to the ground state is restricted, hence the fluorescence is quenched as evident from Fluorescence Lifetime and UV–Visible absorption spectrum [36]. Fe is a redox active element which can possibly bind to the three functional groups of cysteine. The valence electrons present in the –N, –O and –S of cysteine group are hypothesised as capable of forming chemical bonds with Fe leading to the formation of stable inner sphere complexes having Fe–O, Fe–N and Fe–S coordination environments [37,38]. Due to strong affinity of L cysteine with Fe, it takes away Fe from the surface of MCDs, as a result, the fluorescence is recovered as demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

The synthesised chiral carbon dots are characterised using UV–Visible spectroscopy as given in Fig. 3.7 A and B. A sharp absorption peak at 340 nm corresponds to n-π* transition of OH, NH2 and COOH groups present on the surface of CDs [39]. There is change in absorption peaks on addition of Fe indicates the complex formation on the surface of MCDs. On addition of l-Cysteine, it strongly binds to L/D MCDs –Fe2+ system which attributes to absorption peak at 320 nm. The change in UV–Visible absorption spectra further confirms the static quenching mechanism [40].

Fluorescence Lifetime studies of MCDs, Fe and l-cysteine system were conducted using Time Correlated Single Photon Counting System (TCSPC) system by excitation using EPLED 450 Laser diode. There is no appreciable change in average lifetime of l-MCDs (4.42 to 4.40 ns) on addition of Fe. But on addition of l-cysteine, the lifetime increases from (4.40 - 4.54 ns) in l-Methionine CDs. Whereas, in d-Methionine CDs, the lifetime data reveals a static quenching mechanism with no appreciable change in average lifetime from (5.48 to 5.53 ns) on addition of Fe. But on addition of l-cysteine, the lifetime increases from (5.53 to 5.54 ns) only which reveals the “turn-on” behaviour of l-cysteine [41]. The triexponential fluorescence decay curve of l-MCDs and d-MCDs are given in Fig. 3.8 A and B. and Tables S1. and S2 respectively. In order to ensure the reproducibility, the experiments were done in triplicate.

The Stern Volmer plot was used to calculate the relationship between the quenching effect (I0/I) on fluorescent probe with the different concentration of analyte. I0/I = 1+Ksv [Q]. Where, I0 is the intensity of the Methionine carbon dots (MCDs), and I is the intensity at different concentrations of Fe (II) as given in Fig. S6 A and B. The fitted linear data with R2 = 0.99289 and R2 = 0.98565 was calculated for l-Methionine carbon dot (L-MCDs) and d-Methionine carbon dot (D-MCDs) towards the turn-on response of l-Cysteine and a linear plot is obtained as given in Fig. 3.9 A and B. The Limit of Detection (LOD) is calculated using the equation 3σ/K and is obtained as 0.71 µM and 0.94 µM respectively. The comparison table depicting the previous works are tabulated in Table S3 which validates the comparability of detection of l-Cysteine with MCDs in biological samples.

To investigate the practical applicability of the developed probe for the sensitive detection of l-cysteine, selectivity studies were conducted with other coexisting ions and biomarkers etc. such as sodium, potassium, creatine, glucose, urea, zinc, mercury, dopamine, histidine and thiols such as homocysteine and glutathione with 5.35 mM concentration each. Compared to other coexisting biomolecules, the developed sensor shows good “turn-on” response towards l-cysteine. The results reveal the selectivity of probe towards the specific sensing of l-cysteine. The selectivity bar diagram for l-MCDs and d-MCDs towards various analytes are given in Fig. S7 A and B and the interference bar diagram is depicted in Fig. 4 A and B respectively. The fluorescence response of two probes towards d-Cysteine were also evaluated. Interestingly, it is observed that there is infinitesimally small turn-on response for l-MCDs and d-MCDs with d-Cysteine. Moreover l-MCDs-Fe (II) system was irresponsive towards the detection of d-Cysteine as given in Fig. S8 A and B. In human body, the existence of l-chiral forms is more abundant and clinically significant. Hence, the detection of l-Cysteine is more relevant compared to d-Cysteine.

To study the applicability of the probe in biological samples, sensing of l-cysteine was conducted in human blood serum samples. The real sample analysis is conducted in concentrated serum,10 times diluted,100 times diluted serum samples. The recovery percentage was calculated for different additions in the three samples as given in Table 1 . The recovery percentage was found to lie between 86.34% to 104.09% suggesting that the synthesised carbon dot is an excellent fluorescent probe in sensing l-cysteine from human blood serum samples.

To evaluate the practical use of the probe in the detection of l-Cysteine, a paper strip experiment was conducted. For this, three pieces of equal sized Whatman No.40.filter paper was cut. Each of the strip is soaked in MCDs solution (0.8 mg/mL) separately and dried in air. Then, observed under UV irradiation and blue fluorescence of the strip was observed. After that, strip B is soaked in solution of Iron chloride (1.2 mM) and dried. Then placed under UV irradiation. There is a decrease in luminescence in strip B. No visible colour change is observed under day light. Again, the strip is soaked in 3.45 mM of l-Cysteine, and it is observed that the fluorescence is enhanced as shown in Fig. 4.1 (a–d). A blank pristine unsoaked filter paper is included as strip d which does not show any fluorescence under UV light. Thus, the developed probe can be used for the sensing of l-cysteine.

A blue, fluorescent chiral CDs (L and D Methionine CDs) were prepared by microwave assisted method using Citric acid, urea and L/D methionine as the precursors with emission observed in the range of 445-450 nm with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The addition of 0.53 mM Fe (II) has quenched the fluorescence of synthesised L/D methionine CDs and recovered the fluorescence on addition of 3.45 mM l-Cysteine. The fluorescence response of L/D-MCDs with other metal ions and biomolecules were studied. The developed sensor shows good selectivity and sensitivity towards the detection of l-Cysteine. A paper strip assay was also developed for studying practical applicability. The Limit of Detection (LOD) for l-MCDs and d-MCDs was obtained as 0.71 µM and 0.94 µM respectively. The sensor shows good recovery percentage ranging from 86.34% to 104.09% in spiked human serum sample. Thereby, we have developed a “Turn – on” sensor based on Chiral L/D Methionine carbon dots for the detection of l-Cysteine in biological samples. Interestingly, the developed probes show negligible turn-on response towards d-Cysteine. This irresponsiveness of the l-MCDs and d-MCDs probes towards d-Cysteine reminds us of the nature evolved preference towards l-amino acids in living systems.

The experiments were done in accordance with the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) ethical standards and were permitted by the University-level Human Ethical Committee Order no: (ULECRIHS/UOK/2019/48, University of Kerala, Thiruvananthapuram). All the healthy volunteers who donated the blood and urine samples were provided consent to participate and publish the result.
No funding was received for conducting the study.

Merin K Abraham: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jithin Raj R: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Anju S Madanan: Writing – review & editing. Susan Varghese: Writing – review & editing. Ali Ibrahim Shkhair: Writing – review & editing. Geneva Indongo: Writing – review & editing. Greeshma Rajeevan: Writing – review & editing. N.S. Vijila: Writing – review & editing. B.K. Arathy: Writing – review & editing. Sony George: Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author thanks the Head, Department of Chemistry, University of Kerala, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala for the support, guidance, and instrumental facilities. We are also thankful to CSIR-NIIST, Thiruvananthapuram and CLIF, University of Kerala for providing instrumental facilities. We also thank the International Interuniversity Centre for Sensing and Imaging (IIUCSI), Advanced Research Laboratory for Molecular Sensing and Imaging, State Plan Fund 2020–21, University of Kerala for the sophisticated instrumental analysis offered.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.138333.
